design_philosophy.core_loop: Traverse surreal, genre-shifting levels while collecting power-ups and weapons to save Princess What's-Her-Name from Psy-Crow. Each level reinvents the gameplay formula entirely.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Unpredictability and tonal whiplash—players never know what genre or mechanic they'll encounter next (run-and-gun vs. trivia vs. isometric shooter vs. balloon physics). This creates constant cognitive challenge beyond mechanical difficulty.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing an absurdist worm superhero in a universe where logic is optional and the game itself is a surrealist artwork. Embracing creative chaos rather than mastery.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Earthworm Jim 2 deliberately rejects coherence. Rather than iterating on the first game's formula, it *explodes* it into wildly different genre experiments within a single campaign. The game prioritizes 'strangeness as a feature' and refuses to respect player expectations—the final twist reveals all characters are cows. This is maximalist absurdism as design philosophy: weirdness IS the game.

difficulty.lives_system: Lives-based system present; death penalizes player progression. No password system documented, requiring continuous play or use of level select cheat.
difficulty.continues_system: Not explicitly documented in sources; likely limited continues given era and 'no real password system' note suggesting checkpoint scarcity.
difficulty.checkpointing: Minimal checkpointing implied—'long' game with 'no real password system' indicates players must complete levels in single sittings or use hidden level select cheat code.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can access invincibility via cheat menu (Select, Left, Right, A, X, X, Left, Right during pause), level select, and map view mode. Cheats available reduce frustration from lack of save system, suggesting developers acknowledged difficulty pain points.

progression.upgrade_system: Power-up based: chip butty (increases suit power to 200%), multiple gun types (auto-aiming gun, Barn Blaster that clears screen), Snott (friend mechanic for swinging/parachuting). No persistent RPG-style upgrade tree.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear level progression. New mechanics and power-ups introduced per level rather than unlocked through resource gathering. Level-specific gimmicks (Snott in some levels, balloon head in others, cave salamander disguise in Villi People) act as per-stage unlocks.
replayability_hooks: Hidden rooms (cheat tip references 'hidden room in level ATE')
Television items that change level palettes (mentioned in US manual, though not found in any level)
Multiple gun types to discover and experiment with
Genre variety means each level plays like a different game, encouraging re-engagement

world_building_techniques: Absurdist environmental design (intestinal passages, inflated head sequences, isometric islands)
Character-driven humor through level names and visual gags (Villi People, Inflated Head, The Flyin' King)
Surrealist twist ending (all characters revealed as cows in disguise)
Tone through level progression—story emerges from gameplay chaos rather than exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters are level-specific genre experiments rather than traditional 'boss fights.' Each climactic encounter uses the level's unique mechanic (trivia in Villi People, isometric balloon defense vs Major Mucus in Flyin' King). Final encounter is a race against Psy-Crow, not a combat duel—subverts player expectations of boss design.
notable_bosses: Major Mucus (The Flyin' King level—isometric shooter format, requires balloon defense)
Psy-Crow (final level 'See Jim Run Run Jim Run'—obstacle course race rather than combat)
Evil the Cat (Inflated Head level—attacks while Jim floats as balloon)
Implied trivia boss in Villi People (nonsensical multiple-choice questions with no correct answers)

faction_indicators: Princess What's-Her-Name (rescue objective, associated with heroism)
Evil the Cat (chaotic agent, attacks indiscriminately)
danger_signals: Exploding sheep hazards in intestinal passages
Sharp objects that burst balloon head
Enemy attacks during vulnerable movement states
Environmental shifts and distortions (villi proximity effects, palette shifts)
environmental_storytelling: Level geometry reflects theme (intestinal passages in Villi People, isometric island in Flyin King)
Object placement tells micro-narratives (two pigs + one platform = raised plug, per cheat tip)
Palette shifts via TV items alter perception of otherwise dull environments
Game show set design in trivia section suggests artificial/constructed world
Cow disguise twist recontextualizes all visual environments retroactively

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor: Earthworm Jim 2 SNES)
